explanation.title: Working with Text: Strings and Template Literals
explanation.content:
# Strings in JavaScript

A **string** is a sequence of characters used to represent text. Strings are one of the most frequently used types in any program. JavaScript offers three ways to delimit them:

```javascript
const a = 'single quotes';    // traditional
const b = "double quotes";    // traditional
const c = `template literal`; // modern — backticks
```

All three store text equally well. Template literals (backticks) are the modern preference because they support interpolation and multi-line text.

## String Concatenation vs Template Literals

```javascript
const name  = "Rahul";
const score = 92;

// Old way — concatenation with +
console.log("Hello, " + name + "! Score: " + score);

// Modern way — template literal
console.log(`Hello, ${name}! Score: ${score}`);
```

Template literals are cleaner: you embed any expression inside `${}` and it is evaluated and inserted automatically.

## Essential String Properties and Methods

| Property/Method | What it does | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `.length` | Character count | `"hello".length` → `5` |
| `.toUpperCase()` | All uppercase | `"hello".toUpperCase()` → `"HELLO"` |
| `.toLowerCase()` | All lowercase | `"HELLO".toLowerCase()` → `"hello"` |
| `.trim()` | Remove surrounding whitespace | `" hi ".trim()` → `"hi"` |
| `.includes(s)` | Check if substring present | `"hello".includes("ell")` → `true` |
| `.startsWith(s)` | Check prefix | `"hello".startsWith("he")` → `true` |
| `.indexOf(s)` | Position of first match | `"hello".indexOf("l")` → `2` |
| `.slice(s, e)` | Extract substring | `"hello".slice(1, 4)` → `"ell"` |
| `.replace(a, b)` | Replace first match | `"cats".replace("cats","dogs")` → `"dogs"` |
| `.split(sep)` | Split into array | `"a,b,c".split(",")` → `["a","b","c"]` |

## Strings Are Immutable

You cannot change a character inside a string — string methods always return a **new** string without modifying the original:

```javascript
const word = "hello";
const upper = word.toUpperCase(); // new string
console.log(word);   // "hello" — unchanged
console.log(upper);  // "HELLO"
```

In the code example below, you will see string methods and template literals applied to an email address validator.

codeExample.title: Email and Name Formatter
codeExample.explanation:
- `.trim()` — removes all leading and trailing whitespace without affecting internal spaces
- `.split(" ")` — splits the string on every space, producing an array of words
- `.map(w => w[0].toUpperCase() + w.slice(1))` — capitalises the first letter of each word
- `.join(" ")` — reassembles the array back into a single string with spaces between words
- `.indexOf("@")` — returns the integer position of the first `@`; used to know where to slice
- `.includes("@") && .includes(".")` — combines two boolean checks with `&&`

exercise.title: Build a Username Generator
exercise.instructions:
Given the variable fullName = '  Rahul Sharma  ', create a username by: (1) trimming whitespace, (2) converting to lowercase, (3) replacing all spaces with underscores using replaceAll(). Print the original trimmed name and the generated username on separate labelled lines.

Expected output:
Original: Rahul Sharma
Username: rahul_sharma
hints: 💡 Use lower.replaceAll(" ", "_") to replace every space with an underscore and store it as username.
💡 replaceAll() replaces every occurrence; replace() only replaces the first match.
💡 The trimmed name and lowercase steps are already done — username only needs the replaceAll() step applied to lower.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of template literals over string concatenation?
options: Template literals are faster to execute
Template literals support embedded expressions with ${} and multi-line text
Template literals do not require quotes
Template literals automatically convert numbers to strings
questions.explanation: Template literals (backtick strings) support two features concatenation cannot match easily: expression interpolation with ${} — which embeds any expression directly in the string — and multi-line strings without escape characters. Both make code more readable and less error-prone.

questions.question: What does "hello".slice(1, 4) return?
questions.explanation: slice(start, end) extracts characters from index start up to but not including index end. For "hello", index 1 is 'e', index 2 is 'l', index 3 is 'l' — so slice(1, 4) returns "ell". The character at index 4 ('o') is excluded because the end index is non-inclusive.
